Optic radiations involving crossing inferotemporal fibers

text: Which neurotransmitter is primarily involved in visual signal transmission between bipolar and ganglion cells?
rationale: Glutamate is the primary excitatory neurotransmitter used throughout much of the retinal visual pathway.

text: Optic disc pallor following previous optic disc edema indicates which pathophysiologic change?
rationale: Optic atrophy develops following axonal loss and gliosis, resulting in pallor of the optic nerve head.
choices: Loss of retinal ganglion cell axons and gliosis
Persistent axoplasmic stasis and scar formation

text: Which structure provides the primary resistance to aqueous outflow in the conventional pathway?
rationale: Most aqueous outflow resistance is located within the juxtacanalicular connective tissue adjacent to Schlemm's canal.

text: Aqueous humor exits the anterior chamber primarily through which two routes?
rationale: The trabecular pathway accounts for most aqueous drainage, while the uveoscleral pathway provides an alternative route.
choices: Canal of Petit and Canal of Hannover

text: Which region of the optic nerve head is first affected by glaucomatous damage?
rationale: The lamina cribrosa is the primary site of axonal injury and remodeling in glaucomatous optic neuropathy.

text: Elevated intraocular pressure causes optic nerve damage primarily through which mechanism?
rationale: Mechanical deformation of the lamina cribrosa and impaired axoplasmic transport contribute to retinal ganglion cell death.
choices: Axoplasmic flow obstruction and laminar deformation

text: Which of the following is TRUE regarding diurnal variation of intraocular pressure (IOP)?
rationale: Many individuals demonstrate their highest IOP measurements during the early morning hours.
choices: IOP tends to peak in the early morning hours
IOP is lowest in the morning
IOP remains constant throughout the day
IOP variation occurs only in myopes

text: Which finding best distinguishes primary open-angle glaucoma from angle-closure glaucoma?
rationale: Primary open-angle glaucoma occurs despite a gonioscopically open angle and is characterized by progressive optic neuropathy.
choices: Open, normal-appearing angle with elevated IOP and optic neuropathy
Intermittent shallowing of anterior chamber with normal IOP
Sudden painful vision loss with mid-dilated pupil
Congenital corneal edema and tearing in the absence of infection

text: Which diagnostic finding is MOST characteristic of early glaucomatous visual field loss?
rationale: Early glaucomatous visual field defects follow retinal nerve fiber layer anatomy and commonly present as paracentral scotomas, nasal steps, or small arcuate defects respecting the horizontal meridian.

text: Normal-tension glaucoma may result from which contributing mechanism?
rationale: Normal-tension glaucoma is thought to involve vascular dysregulation, reduced optic nerve perfusion, and increased susceptibility of the optic nerve despite statistically normal IOP.
choices: Vascular dysregulation or reduced optic nerve perfusion pressure
Iris bombe formation and increased IOP

text: In primary angle-closure glaucoma, which anatomical feature predisposes to pupillary block?
rationale: Hyperopic eyes with short axial lengths often have crowded anterior segments, increasing the likelihood of pupillary block and angle closure.
choices: Shallow anterior chamber and narrow angle due to short axial length
Deep chamber with flat iris plane and hyperopic prescription
Large anterior chamber volume and neovascularization of the angle
Thin cornea and long axial length

text: Which imaging modality best quantifies retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) thinning in glaucoma?
rationale: Optical coherence tomography provides objective quantitative measurements of RNFL and ganglion cell complex thickness.

text: Which structure connects Schlemm's canal to the episcleral venous system?
rationale: Collector channels and aqueous veins carry aqueous humor from Schlemm's canal into the episcleral venous circulation.

text: Which medication class lowers intraocular pressure primarily by increasing uveoscleral outflow?
rationale: Prostaglandin analogs increase uveoscleral outflow through remodeling of extracellular matrix within the ciliary body.

text: Which acute symptom complex is most consistent with an angle-closure crisis?
rationale: Acute angle closure typically presents with sudden elevation of IOP, severe ocular pain, corneal edema, halos around lights, and a mid-dilated fixed pupil.
choices: Severe ocular pain, halos, mid-dilated fixed pupil, corneal edema
Bilateral gradual visual field loss despite lack of pain

text: Which finding differentiates secondary open-angle glaucoma due to pseudoexfoliation syndrome?
rationale: Pseudoexfoliation syndrome is characterized by deposition of fibrillar material on the lens capsule and within the trabecular meshwork, often accompanied by heavy angle pigmentation.
choices: Flaky material on lens capsule and trabecular pigmentation

text: Which inherited condition is characterized by abnormal anterior chamber angle development in infancy?
rationale: Primary congenital glaucoma results from developmental abnormalities of the trabecular meshwork and Schlemm's canal, causing elevated IOP early in life.

text: Which surgical procedure creates a new fistula from the anterior chamber to the subconjunctival space to enhance aqueous outflow?
rationale: Trabeculectomy creates a guarded filtration pathway allowing aqueous humor to drain into a subconjunctival bleb.

text: Which optic nerve head feature is most specific for glaucomatous damage rather than physiologic cupping?
rationale: Localized neuroretinal rim notching and violation of the ISNT rule are characteristic findings of glaucomatous optic neuropathy.
choices: Large but symmetric cup in both eyes
Pink neuroretinal rim with small cup

text: A patient presents with sudden severe eye pain after hammering metal. What is the most appropriate first diagnostic step?
rationale: CT of the orbits without contrast is the preferred imaging study for suspected metallic intraocular foreign bodies. MRI is contraindicated when a metallic foreign body is possible.
choices: CT scan of the orbits without contrast
MRI of the brain and orbits

text: Which finding best differentiates an open-globe injury from a superficial laceration?
rationale: A positive Seidel test demonstrates aqueous leakage through a full-thickness corneal or scleral wound, confirming an open globe.

text: In orbital floor fracture, diplopia occurs most commonly due to entrapment of which muscle?
rationale: The inferior rectus is most commonly entrapped in orbital floor fractures, producing restricted elevation and vertical diplopia.

text: A patient with blunt trauma shows blood layering in the anterior chamber. Which complication must be closely monitored?
rationale: Red blood cells can obstruct the trabecular meshwork, causing significant secondary elevation of intraocular pressure.
choices: Secondary IOP spike from trabecular blockage

text: Which sign is most concerning for orbital compartment syndrome after trauma?
rationale: Retrobulbar hemorrhage causing acute proptosis, elevated orbital pressure, and decreased vision requires emergent decompression.
choices: Proptosis with tight lids and decreased vision

text: Chemical alkali injuries damage the cornea primarily by which mechanism?
rationale: Alkali substances saponify cell membrane lipids, causing liquefactive necrosis and rapid deep penetration into ocular tissues.
choices: Saponification of cell membranes and deep tissue penetration
Coagulation necrosis with surface barrier formation

text: Which immediate management step takes precedence in chemical ocular burns?
rationale: Immediate copious irrigation is the most important intervention and should occur before any other examination procedures.
choices: Copious irrigation until physiologic pH is achieved

text: A 65-year-old with temporal headache and jaw claudication presents with sudden monocular vision loss. Which laboratory test is most urgent?
rationale: ESR and CRP are rapidly obtainable inflammatory markers that help identify giant cell arteritis and guide immediate treatment.
choices: Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP)

text: After a blow-out fracture, the globe appears sunken with enophthalmos. Which anatomic defect explains this finding?
rationale: Orbital floor fractures allow orbital fat and soft tissues to herniate into the maxillary sinus, increasing orbital volume and producing enophthalmos.
choices: Loss of orbital support due to herniation of fat into the maxillary sinus
Superior wall collapse into frontal sinus
Medial wall fracture into ethmoid cells allowing infection to spread
Zygomatic arch fracture alone resulting in total globe destabilization

text: Which finding indicates optic nerve avulsion following severe blunt ocular trauma?
rationale: Optic nerve avulsion produces profound, immediate vision loss with a marked relative afferent pupillary defect and often little anterior segment pathology.
choices: Profound vision loss with absent afferent pupillary response
Optic disc swelling with pain on movement
Mild field defect and optic nerve pallor with normal vision

text: Which autonomic receptor subtype most directly mediates pupillary dilation via the iris dilator muscle?
condition: Lens / Cataract / IOL / Pre- & Post-Op Care
rationale: The iris dilator muscle is innervated by the sympathetic nervous system and contracts through α1-adrenergic receptor stimulation.

text: Which vitamin is essential for rhodopsin regeneration and night vision?
rationale: Vitamin A is converted into 11-cis-retinal, which combines with opsin to regenerate rhodopsin after photobleaching.

text: Which systemic condition classically causes granulomatous anterior uveitis with elevated ACE and bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy?
rationale: Sarcoidosis produces noncaseating granulomas and is a classic cause of granulomatous anterior uveitis with elevated ACE and hilar lymphadenopathy.

text: Ethambutol toxicity most typically presents with which ocular finding?
rationale: Ethambutol-induced optic neuropathy classically affects the papillomacular bundle, producing bilateral cecocentral scotomas and dyschromatopsia.
choices: Bilateral cecocentral scotomas and red-green dyschromatopsia
Arcuate field loss with normal color vision

text: Which antimalarial dosing threshold minimizes risk of hydroxychloroquine maculopathy?
rationale: Current screening recommendations advise maintaining hydroxychloroquine dosing at or below 5 mg/kg/day based on actual body weight.

text: Which systemic medication can precipitate acute myopic shift and secondary angle closure from choroidal effusions?
rationale: Topiramate may induce ciliochoroidal effusions, causing anterior displacement of the lens-iris diaphragm with myopic shift and angle closure.

text: Tamoxifen is most associated with which ocular toxicity?
rationale: Tamoxifen toxicity classically produces crystalline maculopathy and may be associated with macular edema.

text: Amiodarone most commonly produces which ocular change?
rationale: Amiodarone commonly causes corneal verticillata (vortex keratopathy), which is usually visually insignificant.

text: Which systemic antibiotic is relatively safe in pregnancy for ocular infections?
rationale: Penicillins such as amoxicillin are generally considered safe during pregnancy, whereas tetracyclines are avoided.

text: Which endocrine disease leads to fluctuating diplopia, lid retraction, and restrictive strabismus due to extraocular muscle fibrosis?
rationale: Thyroid eye disease causes autoimmune inflammation and fibrosis of extraocular muscles, especially the inferior rectus, leading to restrictive diplopia and lid retraction.

text: In diabetes mellitus, which mechanism most directly causes diabetic macular edema?
rationale: Pericyte loss and endothelial dysfunction lead to breakdown of the inner blood-retinal barrier and leakage of fluid into the macula.
choices: Breakdown of inner blood-retinal barrier with capillary leakage

text: Giant cell arteritis threatens vision primarily by ischemia of which structure?
rationale: Arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy results from inflammation and occlusion of the posterior ciliary arteries supplying the optic nerve head.

text: Which two labs are most immediately useful when suspecting giant cell arteritis?
rationale: ESR and CRP are rapidly available inflammatory markers commonly elevated in giant cell arteritis.

text: Which HIV-related opportunistic infection most commonly causes necrotizing retinitis with granular borders and hemorrhage?
rationale: CMV retinitis is the most common opportunistic retinal infection in advanced HIV/AIDS and produces characteristic hemorrhagic necrotizing retinitis.

text: Which systemic drug interaction increases INR and bleeding risk with warfarin and may present with subconjunctival hemorrhages?
rationale: Macrolide antibiotics and azole antifungals inhibit warfarin metabolism, increasing anticoagulant effect and bleeding risk.

text: Which inherited hemoglobinopathy increases risk of IOP spikes and optic nerve damage with hyphema?
rationale: Sickle cell disease and trait promote sickling of erythrocytes within the trabecular meshwork, impairing aqueous outflow and increasing IOP.

text: Which systemic antibiotic class should be avoided in children due to risk of tooth discoloration and bone effects?
rationale: Tetracyclines bind calcium in developing teeth and bone, causing discoloration and impaired skeletal development.

text: Which neurologic disease most commonly presents with painful unilateral optic neuritis in young adults?
rationale: Optic neuritis is strongly associated with multiple sclerosis and may be its presenting manifestation.

text: Which test confirms neurosyphilis in a patient with uveitis and positive treponemal screening?
rationale: CSF VDRL obtained through lumbar puncture is the standard confirmatory test supporting neurosyphilis.
choices: ANA titer with sacroiliac joint X-ray

text: Which systemic drug is a strong risk factor for IFIS (intraoperative floppy iris syndrome)?
rationale: Tamsulosin blocks α1A receptors in the iris dilator muscle, increasing the risk of intraoperative floppy iris syndrome during cataract surgery.

text: Which cardiac medication class can cause xanthopsia (yellow vision) and dyschromatopsia?
condition: Lens / Cataract / IOL / Pre- & Post-Op Care
rationale: Digoxin toxicity classically produces yellow vision, color vision disturbances, and visual phenomena such as halos.

text: Which systemic antifungal used for onychomycosis can cause optic neuritis and should prompt ocular review if vision symptoms arise?
rationale: Voriconazole is associated with neuro-ophthalmic side effects including visual disturbances and, rarely, optic neuropathy or optic neuritis.

text: Which vitamin deficiency is associated with Bitot spots and xerophthalmia?
rationale: Vitamin A deficiency causes conjunctival xerosis, Bitot spots, night blindness, and severe ocular surface disease.

text: Which systemic antihypertensive is relatively contraindicated in asthmatics due to bronchospasm risk and can mask hypoglycemia?
condition: Lens / Cataract / IOL / Pre- & Post-Op Care
rationale: Nonselective β-blockers may precipitate bronchospasm by blocking β2 receptors and can mask adrenergic warning signs of hypoglycemia.

text: Which systemic diuretic is a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor used to lower IOP but is cautioned in sickle cell disease?
rationale: Acetazolamide may promote systemic acidosis, which can increase erythrocyte sickling in susceptible patients.

text: Interferon-α therapy for hepatitis can cause which retinal change?
rationale: Interferon-associated retinopathy commonly presents with cotton-wool spots and retinal hemorrhages due to microvascular ischemia.

text: Linezolid prolonged therapy is associated with which neuro-ophthalmic complication?
rationale: Linezolid can cause mitochondrial toxicity leading to bilateral optic neuropathy, especially with prolonged use.

text: Which systemic disease is most associated with episcleritis that blanches with phenylephrine and is often benign?
rationale: Episcleritis is commonly associated with autoimmune diseases, particularly inflammatory bowel disease and rheumatoid arthritis.

text: Wilson disease can show which distinctive corneal sign due to copper deposition?
rationale: Copper deposition within Descemet's membrane produces the characteristic golden-brown Kayser-Fleischer ring.

text: Which systemic medication can exacerbate dry eye by reducing aqueous secretion through anticholinergic effects?
rationale: Anticholinergic burden reduces lacrimation and salivation.

text: Which vitamin toxicity can cause pseudotumor cerebri (IIH) symptoms including papilledema?
rationale: Hypervitaminosis A is a known trigger for IIH.

text: Which endocrine disorder is most associated with bitemporal hemianopia from chiasmal compression?
rationale: Pituitary macroadenomas compress decussating nasal fibers at the chiasm.

text: Sildenafil and related PDE-5 inhibitors have been associated (rarely) with which optic event?
rationale: An association with NAION is reported; counsel patients with crowded discs and vascular risk.

text: Which systemic condition most classically causes salt-and-pepper fundus changes?
rationale: Congenital rubella syndrome may show mottled RPE (salt-and-pepper).

text: Which inherited thrombophilia increases risk for retinal vein occlusion and should be considered in young patients?
rationale: Inherited thrombophilias such as Factor V Leiden predispose to venous thromboembolic disease and may contribute to retinal vein occlusion in younger patients.

text: Which systemic antitubercular regimen component is most associated with optic neuropathy risk?
rationale: Ethambutol dose and duration correlate with optic neuropathy risk.

text: Which autoimmune disease is most associated with peripheral ulcerative keratitis (PUK) and scleritis?
rationale: RA vasculitis can cause PUK and necrotizing scleritis.

text: Which systemic lipid disorder classically produces a corneal arcus at a young age?
rationale: Early arcus in patients <40 suggests significant hypercholesterolemia.

text: Which antibiotic pair is preferred for classic toxoplasma chorioretinitis?
rationale: Classic ocular toxoplasmosis therapy consists of pyrimethamine and sulfadiazine with folinic acid (leucovorin) supplementation to reduce hematologic toxicity.

text: Which systemic medication commonly causes whorl keratopathy besides amiodarone?
rationale: Chloroquine and other cationic amphiphiles cause verticillata.

text: Which neurologic autoimmune disorder causes fatigable ptosis and diplopia with positive ice-pack test?
rationale: MG targets postsynaptic ACh receptors; ocular symptoms are common.

text: Which endocrine disorder is associated with cotton-wool spots, microaneurysms, and neovascularization?
rationale: Chronic hyperglycemia damages capillaries leading to diabetic retinopathy.

text: Which topical glaucoma medication class commonly causes conjunctival hyperemia, eyelash growth, and periorbital fat atrophy?
rationale: Topical prostaglandin analogs commonly cause conjunctival hyperemia, increased eyelash growth, iris pigmentation changes, and prostaglandin-associated periorbitopathy.

text: Which neurologic condition presents with increased ICP, papilledema, headache, and transient visual obscurations, commonly in young obese females?
rationale: IIH (pseudotumor) features elevated opening pressure with normal imaging.

text: Which thyroid medication overdose can cause lid lag and stare with sympathetic overactivity signs?
rationale: Excess thyroid hormone increases sympathetic tone causing lid retraction and lid lag.

text: Which systemic malignancy is notorious for choroidal metastasis presenting as creamy subretinal lesions?
rationale: Breast and lung carcinomas are the most common primary tumors metastasizing to the choroid.

text: Which connective tissue disorder is associated with lens subluxation and increased axial length?
rationale: Fibrillin-1 defects lead to superotemporal ectopia lentis and increased axial length.

text: Which systemic drug is contraindicated in pregnancy and can cause fetal renal anomalies and skull defects?
rationale: ACE inhibitors and ARBs are teratogenic, particularly during the second and third trimesters.

text: Which autoimmune disease classically produces dry eye with parotid enlargement and positive anti-Ro/SSA?
rationale: Lacrimal and salivary gland infiltration causes aqueous-deficient dry eye in Sjögren syndrome.

text: Which systemic agent increases uveoscleral outflow when used topically as first-line glaucoma therapy?
rationale: Topical prostaglandin analogs increase uveoscleral outflow by remodeling the extracellular matrix of the ciliary muscle.

text: Which HLA-B27-associated systemic disease is most strongly linked with recurrent acute anterior uveitis?
rationale: Ankylosing spondylitis is the classic HLA-B27-associated systemic disease linked to recurrent acute nongranulomatous anterior uveitis.

text: Which systemic antiplatelet/anticoagulant combination raises risk of ocular hemorrhage during intravitreal injections?
rationale: Combined anticoagulation and antiplatelet therapy increases bleeding risk compared with either therapy alone.

text: Which systemic disorder is associated with Lisch nodules on the iris and optic pathway gliomas?
rationale: Neurofibromatosis type 1 is characterized by café-au-lait macules, neurofibromas, Lisch nodules, and optic pathway gliomas.

text: Which hepatic disease causes conjunctival and scleral icterus due to bilirubin deposition?
rationale: Elevated bilirubin deposits in elastin-rich tissues including the sclera, producing icterus.

text: Which pulmonary disease therapy can cause raised IOP and posterior subcapsular cataracts with chronic use?
rationale: Chronic corticosteroid use can elevate IOP in steroid responders and predispose patients to posterior subcapsular cataracts.

text: Which renal disease finding is associated with cotton-wool spots due to hypertension?
rationale: Chronic kidney disease is commonly associated with hypertension, which may produce hypertensive retinopathy with cotton-wool spots and retinal hemorrhages.
choices: Hypertensive retinopathy from chronic kidney disease
Polycystic kidney disease never shows this

text: Which endocrine emergency presents with Kussmaul respirations, dehydration, and blurred vision from hyperosmolarity?
rationale: Diabetic ketoacidosis causes metabolic acidosis, dehydration, and hyperglycemia that can induce transient refractive changes.

text: Which systemic condition is classically associated with Roth spots (white-centered retinal hemorrhages)?
rationale: Roth spots are classically associated with infective endocarditis, although they may also occur in leukemia, anemia, and other systemic diseases.

text: Which neurologic infectious disease causes Argyll Robertson pupils (light-near dissociation)?
rationale: Argyll Robertson pupils accommodate but do not react to light and are classically associated with neurosyphilis.

text: Which systemic cancer therapy is most associated with MEK-inhibitor retinopathy (SRF at the macula)?
rationale: MEK inhibitors can produce reversible serous maculopathy.

text: Which inherited retinal degeneration causes night blindness and progressive constricted visual fields with bone-spicule pigmentation?
rationale: Retinitis pigmentosa typically begins with rod photoreceptor dysfunction, causing nyctalopia and progressive peripheral visual field loss before cone involvement.

text: Which hematologic malignancy can cause hyperviscosity retinopathy with dilated tortuous veins and hemorrhages?
rationale: IgM paraproteinemia increases serum viscosity causing retinal changes.

text: Which endocrine therapy can cause color vision changes and an optic-neuritis-like picture (rare)?
rationale: Tamoxifen has multiple ocular toxicities beyond crystalline maculopathy.

text: Which systemic antibiotics are most associated with risk of tendinopathy and may precipitate uveitis (rare reports)?
rationale: Fluoroquinolones carry a boxed warning for tendinopathy; uveitis is a rare reported adverse effect.

text: Which systemic immunotherapy can produce uveitis and serous retinal detachments as an adverse event?
rationale: Immune checkpoint inhibitors can cause ocular immune-related adverse events including uveitis and serous retinal detachments.

text: Which systemic deficiency state causes optic neuropathy with central scotomas and anemia?
rationale: Vitamin B12 deficiency can produce optic neuropathy with central or cecocentral scotomas and megaloblastic anemia.

text: Which pediatric intraocular tumor is classically associated with leukocoria and requires urgent evaluation?
rationale: Leukocoria in a child should be considered retinoblastoma until proven otherwise because delayed diagnosis can be life-threatening.

text: Which systemic autoimmune disease shows a malar rash and can cause cotton-wool spots and vaso-occlusive retinopathy?
rationale: Immune complex vasculitis in systemic lupus erythematosus can produce retinal ischemia, hemorrhages, and vaso-occlusive disease.

text: Which systemic metabolic disorder classically causes a cherry-red spot at the macula in infants?
rationale: GM2 gangliosidosis in Tay-Sachs disease causes retinal ganglion cell accumulation that spares the fovea, producing a cherry-red spot.

text: Which medication class is relatively contraindicated in patients with anatomically narrow angles because its anticholinergic effects may precipitate angle closure?
rationale: Anticholinergic medications, including many antihistamines, can induce mydriasis and precipitate acute angle closure in susceptible individuals.

text: Which systemic disease produces Langerhans cell histiocytosis with possible orbital masses and proptosis in children?
rationale: Langerhans cell histiocytosis can involve the orbit, producing lytic bone lesions, orbital masses, and proptosis.

text: Which vitamin deficiency causes perifollicular hemorrhages and delayed wound healing that may worsen subconjunctival hemorrhages?
rationale: Vitamin C is required for collagen synthesis; deficiency weakens blood vessel walls and connective tissue.

text: Which systemic corticosteroid can increase IOP and increase the risk of secondary open-angle glaucoma with chronic use?
rationale: Chronic corticosteroid use can increase trabecular meshwork outflow resistance and elevate IOP in susceptible individuals.

text: Which neurologic syndrome causes rapid ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, and areflexia following infection?
rationale: Miller Fisher syndrome is a variant of Guillain-Barré syndrome characterized by the triad of ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, and areflexia.

text: Which systemic endocrine abnormality causes thickened extraocular muscles with tendon sparing on imaging?
rationale: Thyroid eye disease classically produces fusiform enlargement of extraocular muscle bellies with relative tendon sparing.

text: Which systemic drug is safest for treating acute bacterial sinusitis in a pregnant patient with preseptal cellulitis risk?
rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is generally considered safe during pregnancy and is commonly used for bacterial sinusitis.

text: Which systemic immunosuppressant blocks calcineurin to reduce IL-2 transcription and is used in transplant patients with possible ocular side effects (e.g., tremor, neurotoxicity)?
rationale: Tacrolimus inhibits calcineurin, reducing IL-2 transcription and T-cell activation.

text: Which systemic cancer in men most commonly metastasizes to the choroid?
rationale: Lung carcinoma is the most common source of choroidal metastases in men.

text: Which systemic mitochondrial disease presents with painless central vision loss in young men and temporal disc pallor?
rationale: Leber hereditary optic neuropathy causes mitochondrial dysfunction affecting the papillomacular bundle.

text: Which endocrine disorder is linked to central serous chorioretinopathy through cortisol excess?
rationale: Endogenous hypercortisolism and exogenous corticosteroid exposure are well-established risk factors for central serous chorioretinopathy.

text: Which medication class is most likely to precipitate angle closure by inducing mydriasis in a susceptible patient with narrow angles?
rationale: Anticholinergic medications can induce pupillary dilation and precipitate acute angle closure in anatomically predisposed eyes.

text: Which systemic autoimmune blistering disease shows conjunctival scarring and symblepharon risk?
rationale: Autoantibodies against the epithelial basement membrane produce progressive conjunctival cicatrization and symblepharon formation.

text: Which systemic bone medication has been associated with scleritis and uveitis (rare) and requires cessation if inflammation occurs?
rationale: Bisphosphonates have rare but well-documented ocular inflammatory adverse effects including uveitis and scleritis.

text: Which systemic nutritional deficiency can cause nystagmus, ataxia, and ophthalmoplegia (Wernicke encephalopathy)?
rationale: Thiamine deficiency causes Wernicke encephalopathy, classically presenting with ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, and altered mental status.

text: Which systemic autoimmune disease presents with peripheral ulcerative keratitis and corneal melting?
rationale: Rheumatoid arthritis-associated vasculitis can produce peripheral ulcerative keratitis and progressive corneal melt.

text: Which systemic condition increases risk for nonarteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (NAION)?
rationale: Obstructive sleep apnea is a well-established risk factor for NAION due to nocturnal hypoxia and vascular dysregulation.

text: Which medication or supplement exposure is classically associated with intracranial hypertension (IIH)?
rationale: Hypervitaminosis A and tetracycline-class antibiotics are classic medication-related causes of intracranial hypertension.
choices: Vitamin A excess and tetracycline-class antibiotics

text: Which lysosomal storage disorder causes angiokeratomas, corneal verticillata, and painful neuropathy?
rationale: Fabry disease causes accumulation of globotriaosylceramide resulting in corneal verticillata, angiokeratomas, neuropathy, and systemic vasculopathy.
